description: Enable robust offline-first collaborative editing with deterministic conflict resolution using SyncEngine, SyncManager, SyncQueue and ConflictStrategy.
Provide durable point-in-time snapshots and fast recovery via SnapshotStore abstractions (SnapshotStore, DiskDocumentSnapshotStore, FileSnapshotStore) and DocumentSnapshotService.
Support fast full-text search across documents using InvertedIndex, Indexer and SearchPlugin with IndexStats for monitoring.
Offer real-time peer presence and session tracking through PresenceTracker and PeerPresence to enable collaborative UX features.
Expose extensibility for import/export and custom features through PluginHost, PluginContext, Plugin, ExportPlugin and SearchPlugin.
Provide summarization capabilities that can run locally (LocalSummarizer) and remotely (RemoteSummarizer) while minimizing user-perceived latency.
The codebase is organized under a single module boundary 'signal-app' (app/), with ~36 files forming the application surface.
Persistence is abstracted behind SnapshotStore; concrete implementations include DiskDocumentSnapshotStore and FileSnapshotStore, allowing pluggable storage backends.
Synchronization is implemented as a layered engine: SyncEngine for protocol semantics and SyncManager/SyncQueue for delivery, ack tracking and retry.
Search is separated into indexing and query components: Indexer and WorkerPool build an InvertedIndex; SearchPlugin and SearchQuery provide extensible query handling.
PluginHost centralizes plugin lifecycle and context (PluginContext) allowing plugins (Plugin, ExportPlugin, SearchPlugin) to produce storage events and augment behavior.
Presence and session concerns are decoupled: PeerPresence models peer state while PresenceTracker aggregates and exposes presence events for the app.
LocalSummarizer.summarize is detected as a pure function (no side effects), whereas RemoteSummarizer.summarize performs network IO and is asynchronous.
There is an explicit refactor history replacing Document with DocumentSnapshot to enforce readonly semantics, but both types still exist (Document, DocumentSnapshot).
Multiple storage service variants appear: DocumentStore and DeprecatedDocumentStore co-exist in the graph, suggesting incomplete migration or compatibility layers.
Storage event contracts are explicit (StorageEventCreated, StorageEventUpdated, StorageEventDeleted, StorageEventLinked, StorageEvent) and used by the StorageEventBus contract.
There are explicit contracts for asynchronous validation (DocumentValidatorAsync) and synchronous validation (DocumentValidatorSync), but no central enforcement node visible.

Topology shows duplicated service names (two sets of DocumentSnapshotService, DiskDocumentSnapshotStore, SnapshotStore, DocumentStore, SyncEngine, SyncManager) which may indicate test fixtures vs runtime variants or accidental duplication.
The graph lists IndexStats and InvertedIndexSearchHit contracts indicating operational metrics and search result shaping are first-class concepts.
Decisions for TypeScript adoption and Testing adoption are recorded as decision nodes, implying those are active cross-cutting concerns but their current adoption level is uncertain.

Invariant nodes include error-boundary, guard clauses, input validation, sanitization and rate-limit enforcement, but rate-limit and observability constraints may not be uniformly wired into services like RemoteSummarizer or Indexer.
Authentication required: all operations that mutate or read user-scoped documents must enforce authentication (AppConfig, SignalApp-level policy).
Persistent storage: SnapshotStore-backed snapshots (DiskDocumentSnapshotStore/FileSnapshotStore) are the ground truth and must be durable across restarts.
Observability: Indexer, SyncEngine and SyncManager must emit IndexStats/TelemetryEvent metrics for monitoring and alerting.
Async job processing: WorkerPool and Indexer must respect backpressure and rate-limit invariants to avoid I/O saturation or CPU exhaustion.
Test coverage required: critical protocols (SyncEngine, ConflictStrategy, Snapshot persistence, InvertedIndex) must have regression tests per the 'Test coverage required' constraint.
Under high concurrency the SyncManager's queuing and ConflictStrategy will produce frequent conflict candidates and may reveal missing conflict resolution tests.
DiskDocumentSnapshotStore will become an I/O bottleneck for large-scale indexing or many concurrent snapshot writes unless SnapshotStore implementations batch or offload writes.
Indexer + WorkerPool will show increased latency and high memory use as corpus size grows, potentially causing degraded responsiveness for SearchQuery handling.
RemoteSummarizer will introduce availability and latency variability into summarization features; absent robust timeouts and fallback to LocalSummarizer, UX will degrade.
PluginHost currently lacks strong isolation: a misbehaving plugin (Plugin/ExportPlugin/SearchPlugin) could throw unhandled errors that violate the error-boundary invariant.
Co-existence of DeprecatedDocumentStore and DocumentStore may mask edge cases where older code paths bypass new validation or sanitization rules (DocumentValidatorSync/Async).

Offline-first intent (SyncEngine/OfflineSyncQueue) conflicts with reliance on RemoteSummarizer for key UX: offline mode requires LocalSummarizer fallback yet both implementations differ in side effects.

Desire for a single source-of-truth SnapshotStore contrasts with duplicated service instances (two DocumentSnapshotService/DiskDocumentSnapshotStore entries) suggesting fragmentation or unfinished migration.
Project-level decisions record TypeScript adoption but presence of deprecated entities (DeprecatedDocumentStore) and duplicated services suggests incomplete modernization and mixed paradigms.

Extensibility via PluginHost conflicts with safety requirements (rate-limit, sanitization, error-boundary) because plugins have broad access to StorageEvent and Document contracts and may bypass invariants.
